topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam on the paper, a damning scarlet blotch across the final line. I stared at the 'D-'. It felt like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. My knuckles were white where I gripped the desk, the cheap wood digging into my palms. I slowly pushed the paper away, the sound of the rustling paper loud in the sudden silence of my room. Why? I’d poured weeks into that project, the late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee, the meticulous research. Now, it was just… a failure. A public admission that I wasn't as brilliant as everyone assumed. I stood up, pacing the small confines of my room, my jaw tight. Every glance in the mirror seemed to confirm the disappointment etched onto my face.

[story 2]

"Well, isn't that just marvelous," I muttered, the words tasting like ash. I picked up the graded essay, turning it over and over, tracing the poorly constructed arguments with a fingertip. The professor’s feedback was sparse, almost dismissive—a few pointed critiques that felt like deliberate jabs. A slow, cold knot tightened in my stomach. I didn't bother to look at the syllabus again; the instructions seemed irrelevant now. I slammed the book shut, the sound echoing sharply. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, feeling a profound sense of being underestimated, of having been utterly fooled by my own efforts.

[story 3]

The notification flashed on my screen, stark and unforgiving. A C-. I let out a breath I hadn't realized I was holding, a shaky, humorless sound. My shoulders slumped, the tension draining out of me in a heavy, leaden wave. I didn't even bother to open the email to read the detailed breakdown. Instead, I began methodically sorting through my notes, tearing pages out, the frantic energy of the preceding weeks curdling into a dull, heavy resentment. I picked up a pen, not to write, but just to twist the plastic casing between my fingers, focusing entirely on the small, sharp resistance of the cheap material. This wasn't just a bad grade; it felt like a personal slight, a confirmation that all that striving had amounted to nothing substantial.
